summary: Overall UK risk remains Elevated. External security and economic spillovers from the Middle East conflict continue to be the key system-shaping factor, with the Bank of England explicitly linking war-driven energy price rises to higher near-term inflation and growth risk. Domestically, disruption risk remains concentrated in health service pressure and industrial action rather than acute governance instability, while natural hazards remain mostly localised with a small number of Environment Agency flood alerts active.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: Inflation is still above target and the Bank of England continues to highlight war-driven energy costs as a near-term inflation and growth risk; Brent remains volatile at a level that can feed through to UK fuel and utility costs.
drivers: BoE held Bank Rate at 3.75% (19 March 2026) and warned the Middle East war has raised energy prices, pushing up near-term inflation and potentially slowing growth
ONS CPI inflation was 3.0% y/y in February 2026 (released 25 March 2026)
Brent crude remains a key pass-through risk for UK energy and transport costs

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: The UK terrorism baseline remains SUBSTANTIAL, and external conflict dynamics (Middle East and Russia–Ukraine) continue to create elevated risk of wider spillovers (including maritime security and energy-market impacts).
drivers: MI5 states the UK national terrorism threat level is SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely)
Government has convened crisis coordination on Middle East spillovers (COBR(M) readout on domestic economic impacts)
Ongoing external conflict environment continues to drive security uncertainty

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: Institutions appear to be functioning normally; the main governance pressure evidenced in reviewed official items is policy management of Middle East spillovers (prices, economic resilience) rather than an acute domestic political crisis.
drivers: COBR(M) meeting on 23 March 2026 focused on domestic economic impacts of the Middle East situation and potential measures against price gouging
No new evidence in reviewed sources of immediate UK constitutional or governance instability
Ongoing political attention on household cost pressures and resilience measures

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: Flood risk appears mostly localised, with the Environment Agency flood alert list showing a small number of active alerts and no signal in reviewed sources of a UK-wide severe weather emergency.
drivers: Environment Agency flood-warning service lists 7 active flood alerts (flooding is possible)
Forecast messaging on the EA service indicates low national flood forecast in the near term (subject to change)
No UK-wide severe hazard signal identified in reviewed official summaries

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: Health-service disruption risk remains elevated due to ongoing resident doctors’ strike action in England, with knock-on cancellation and capacity impacts despite mitigation efforts.
drivers: BBC reports resident doctors in England are striking from 07:00 BST on 7 April to 13 April, with disruption expected
NHS service pressure and backlogs remain a resilience concern when combined with industrial action
Cost-of-living pressures remain a background stressor (energy sensitivity noted by BoE)

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: No confirmed UK national-scale outage was identified in reviewed sources, but the advisory environment remains elevated with the NCSC urging urgent mitigation for widely deployed edge products (NetScaler), consistent with ongoing exploitation pressure for internet-facing systems.
drivers: NCSC urged UK organisations to take immediate action to mitigate vulnerabilities affecting Citrix NetScaler ADC/Gateway (CVE-2026-3055, CVE-2026-4368)
Continued global reporting of exploitation of edge/network appliances reinforces elevated baseline cyber risk
Ongoing need for rapid patching and monitoring of internet-facing services

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Trading Economics (April 10, 2026) lists Brent at 96.19 USD/bbl (+0.27% day). https://tradingeconomics.com/commodity/brent-crude-oil

indicators.name: UK CPI inflation (y/y)
indicators.note: ONS reported CPI at 3.0% in the 12 months to February 2026 (release date 25 March 2026). https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/inflationandpriceindices/bulletins/consumerpriceinflation/february2026

indicators.name: Bank Rate (UK)
indicators.note: Bank of England: Bank Rate held at 3.75% (published 19 March 2026); notes Middle East war-driven energy price rises will push up near-term inflation. https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/the-interest-rate-bank-rate

indicators.name: UK terrorism threat level
indicators.note: MI5 states the national terrorism threat level is SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely). https://www.mi5.gov.uk/threats-and-advice/terrorism-threat-levels

indicators.name: Flood alerts (England)
indicators.note: Environment Agency service lists 7 active flood alerts (flooding is possible). https://check-for-flooding.service.gov.uk/alerts-and-warnings

changes_since_last_update: Timestamp advanced to 2026-04-10 05:00 UK and indicators refreshed (Brent, CPI, Bank Rate, threat level, flood alerts).
Brent crude indicator updated from ~US$98/bbl to 96.19 USD/bbl based on Trading Economics front-page quote for April 10.
Social Stability rationale refreshed to reflect ongoing resident doctors’ strike window reported this week.
priority_watch_items: Middle East spillovers: monitor for further disruption to energy supply/shipping and any renewed upward pressure on fuel and utility costs.
UK inflation path: watch for evidence of energy-price pass-through in the next CPI releases and any shift in BoE guidance.
NHS operational resilience: monitor strike impacts (cancellations, critical-incident declarations) and any escalation in wider health-sector disputes.
NetScaler patching: monitor for signs of exploitation and for further NCSC updates on CVE-2026-3055 / CVE-2026-4368 and related edge vulnerabilities.
Flood risk: monitor whether groundwater-related alerts persist or widen after further rainfall.
source_notes: This update prioritises official UK sources (BoE, ONS, MI5, GOV.UK, NCSC) and one commodity pricing reference (Trading Economics) for Brent.
Some indicators are not real-time market feeds; values may differ intraday from trading platforms.

Where evidence is limited to advisory statements rather than confirmed UK incidents (e.g., cyber vulnerabilities), statuses reflect elevated baseline risk rather than a confirmed national-scale outage.
